coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) in aqueous solutions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Di
Jia, Bin
Li, Man
Guo, Jie
Wang, Tieliang
Cao, Cheng
Wang, Yun
Liu, Jihong
coredata.dc:description: A new ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of chromium(VI) under acidic conditions using spirolactamized benzothiazole-substituted N,N-diethylrhodol (Cr6P1) was developed. The probe Cr6P1 showed excellent ratiometric selectivity and high sensitivity for Cr(VI) in acidic solutions, and the detection limit for Cr(VI) was as low as 13 nM (6.9 × 10−4 mg/L). As far as we know, this is the first ratiometric probe based on small organic fluorescence molecules for the detection of Cr(VI). The probe was also applied for detecting Cr(VI) by fluorescent test strips and in the analysis of actual water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr(VI)
ESIPT
Ratiometric
Fluorescence
Oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of probe Cr6P1 by fluorescent test strip
Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21907025), Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province of China (192102310140), Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Henan Academy of Agricultural Sciences (2020CX05). We also thank American Journal Experts (www.aje.com) for its linguistic assistance during the preparation of this manuscript (No. T4NR45ZT).

We also thank American Journal Experts (www.aje.com) for its linguistic assistance during the preparation of this manuscript (No. T4NR45ZT).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra of Cr6P1 (10 μM) in the presence of 10 eq. different metal ions in H2SO4 (10 mM) solution. Inset: naked-eye color changes of probe Cr6P1 with/without Cr(VI).

Fluorescence spectra of Cr6P1 (10 μM) in the presence of 10 eq. different metal ions in H2SO4 (10 mM) solution. λex = 400 nm, scan range 410–700 nm, slit width 5 nm. Inset: fluorogenic color response of probe Cr6P1 with/without Cr(VI) under UV illumination (365 nm).

Fluorescence intensity ratio (F577/F502) of Cr6P1 (10 μM) upon addition of 10 eq. Cr(VI) in the presence of 100 eq. background various coexisting metal ions in H2SO4 (10 mM). λex = 400 nm, scan range 410–700 nm, slit width 5 nm.
Fluorescence emission spectra of Cr6P1 (10 μM) with the gradual addition of various amounts of Cr(VI) (from bottom 0–10 eq.) in H2SO4 (10 mM). λex = 400 nm, scan range 410–700 nm, slit width 5 nm.
Fluorescence intensity ratio (F577/F502) of the linear responses of Cr(VI) concentrations in H2SO4 (10 mM). λex = 400 nm, scan range 410–700 nm, slit width 5 nm.
Synthetic route of the probe Cr6P1.
Ratiometric sensing of Cr6P1 toward Cr(VI).
Determination of Cr(VI) in water samples (n = 6).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aDrinking water is purchased from a local supermarket (Zhengzhou, P.R. China).
bCity tap water is obtained from Zhengzhou city water supply (Henan, P.R. China).
c-eIrrigation waters are obtained from farmland in different counties in Henan province, P.R. China.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) in aqueous solutions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Quality Standards and Testing Technology for Agro-Products, Henan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Zhengzhou 450002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Quality Standards and Testing Technology for Agro-Products

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Bin Jia and Di Zhang contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of chromium(VI) under acidic conditions using spirolactamized benzothiazole-substituted N,N-diethylrhodol (Cr6P1) was developed. The probe Cr6P1 showed excellent ratiometric selectivity and high sensitivity for Cr(VI) in acidic solutions, and the detection limit for Cr(VI) was as low as 13 nM (6.9 × 10−4 mg/L). As far as we know, this is the first ratiometric probe based on small organic fluorescence molecules for the detection of Cr(VI). The probe was also applied for detecting Cr(VI) by fluorescent test strips and in the analysis of actual water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: As a naturally occurring element, chromium (Cr) is widely present in soil, water, and air. Trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) are the two most stable Cr oxidation states [1,2]. Cr(III) has been reported as one of the essential micronutrients in living organisms [3]. Conversely, Cr(VI) is hazardous due to its mutagenicity and carcinogenicity to living organisms, and can affect the normal metabolism of the organism, even make the organism gene mutation [4,5]. Therefore, the concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water is strictly regulated to trace value level by many government regulators. For example, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set a maximum limit of 0.1 mg/L (1.9 μM) for Cr(VI) in drinking water [6]. Therefore, developing efficient methods for the detection of trace amounts of Cr(VI) in drinking and environmental water is very important.

To date, several traditional analytical techniques for detecting Cr(VI) have been reported, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, ion chromatography, and electrochemistry and spectrochemistry methods [7,8]. However, many of these techniques can only detect the total chromium concentration in a sample and cannot distinguish hexavalent chromium from trivalent chromium, while other methods require a complex sample pretreatment process.

Recently, fluorescent probes have attracted great attention due to their advantages, such as high sensitivity, low cost, fast response time, easy operation and real-time monitoring ability [9,10]. A great deal of fluorescent probes have been developed as powerful tools to detect heavy metals and active small molecules in biological and environmental samples [11,12]. Qu et al. developed a fluorescent probe which exhibits satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity for determination of Cr(VI) and Hg2+ in real water samples [13]. Gong et al. reported an “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) [14]. Feng et al. designed a blue emission probe to detect Cr(VI) in milks [15]. Lin et al. synthesized a novel lipid droplet-targeted fluorescent probe for recognition of chromium (VI) in living cells [16].

Although many excellent probes for Cr(VI) have been developed [17–20], these probes mainly focus on changes in the emission intensity of a single wavelength as the only detection signal, resulting in a turn-on or turn-off response. This type of probe is easily disturbed by external environmental factors (pH, instrumental parameters, temperature, and so forth). Ratiometric fluorescent sensors can eliminate most of these interferences by recording internal multiple signal (two or more emission) rate changes, and this ratio provides a built-in correction for external interference factors [21,22]. As far as we know, ratiometric fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) in real samples have not been realized (Table S1).

Herein, we established a novel fluorescent probe (Cr6P1) based on an excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) mechanism for the detection of Cr(VI) in acidic water. Cr6P1 is a rhodamine derivative with an ESIPT unit which displays rhodamine- and 2-(2′-hydroxyphenyl)-benzazoles (HBT)-like emissions in its ‘‘open’’ quinoid form and ‘‘closed’’ spirocyclic form, respectively. In fact, Yang’s group [23] and Zhu’s group [24] have reported using the probe (Cr6P1) to detect Cu2+ and ONOO− in different recognition solution systems, respectively. And both the mechanism are the detected object inducing the opening of the spirolactam ring and then changing the fluorescence signal of the system. It is well known that the selectivity of the same fluorescent probe can be changed by changing the recognition solvent system, which is a new design concept of “single probe can recognize different targets” [25,26]. Interestingly, with the addition of Cr(VI), the probe Cr6P1 affords a ratiometric fluorescent response in acidic solution, which may be due to strong oxidation state of Cr(VI) [27]. Tong's team reported a similar probe (RHB) based on the mechanism that the strong oxidation of Cr(VI) in acid solution (H2SO4, 10 mM) promotes the ring closing structure of rhodamine derivative to open [28]. As we expected, Cr6P1 exhibited highly selective and sensitive recognition of Cr(VI) over other analytes examined in acidic solution. Moreover, the Cr6P1 probe was successfully used to detect Cr(VI) in drinking water and environmental samples.

Fluorescence spectral measurements were performed on a Hitachi spectrophotometer (F-7000 FL, Japan), and both the excitation and emission slit width were set at 5.0 nm. NMR spectra were recorded by a Bruker Avance II (400 MHz), and the ESI-HR-MS analyses were carried out on a Q-Exactive spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The pH measurements were carried out on a Sartorius Delta 320 pH meter (Mettlr-Toledo, Shanghai). All measurements were performed at room temperature, approximately 298 K.

All reagents were used as purchased from commercial companies without further treatment. All the water used in the analysis experiment is distilled water. The metal salts (CrCl3, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, BaCl2, AlCl3, MnCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, CuCl2, ZnCl2, HgCl2, CdCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3 and Pb(NO3)2) were used to study the selectivity and interference properties of the probe. The solutions of Cr(VI) were prepared from K2Cr2O7.

Cr6P1 was synthesized as previously described, as shown in Scheme 1
[23,24]. The detailed synthetic method and the specific characterization data (including 1H NMR, 13C NMR and ESI-HR-MS) of Cr6P1 are described in the supporting information. All the structural characterization data of Cr6P1 were consistent with previously described data.

Cr6P1 was dissolved in ethanol to make a 1 mM stock solution. A H2SO4 (10 mM) solution was the best identification solution system, so this solution system was used throughout the fluorescence spectral experiments.

We first studied the selectivity of the Cr6P1 probe toward various metal ions using an UV–vis spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 1 , Cr6P1 (10 μM) itself exhibited highly absorption in the range of 300–400 nm, however, when adding 10 equivalent Cr(VI), the solution had strong UV absorption between 450 and 600 nm, while other metal ions can’t change the UV absorption spectrum of the solution system. An obvious color change from pale yellow to purple was observed (Fig. 1, inset). The results showed that the probe has a specific UV selectivity for Cr(VI) and could be used as naked eye recognition.

We then used the fluorescence spectrophotometer to study the fluorescence performance of Cr6P1. As shown in Fig. 2 , Cr6P1 (10 μM) itself exhibited an HBT-like emission centered at approximately 502 nm when excited at 400 nm. When 10 equivalent various metal ions, including Cr3+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+ and Ag+, were added to the solution, no significant changes in the fluorescence emission were observed. However, under the same conditions, after the addition of 10 equivalents of Cr(VI), the emission at 502 nm decreased, while the characteristic fluorescence of the delocalized xanthene moiety emission band at 577 nm rapidly increased. And the fluorescent color of the solution changes from green to red under UV illumination (365 nm, Fig. 2 inset). These changes can be attributed to the introduction of Cr(VI) induced opening of the spirocyclic ring of the xanthene moiety, which led to inhibition of the ESIPT process [29]. Notably, Yang's group reported that the probe could recognize Cu2+ in CH3CN:HEPES (30:70, v/v, pH = 7.4, 10 mM) [23]. Therefore, we further investigated the recognition effect of the probe towards different concentrations of Cu2+ (0–300 μM) in H2SO4 (10 mM) solution. The results shown in Fig. S2 demonstrated that the probe has little response to Cu2+ in H2SO4 (10 mM) solution. Therefore, the recognition of Cr(VI) by the probe in acid condition is not interfered by Cu2+. The reason may be that different solvents lead to different objects recognized by probes [30,31].

Moreover, the results of competitive experiments showed that the background metal ions (10 eq. of Cr(VI)) had little interference to the recognition of Cr(VI) in a H2SO4 (10 mM) solution (Fig. 3 ). The interference experiments of various co-existing anions were also evaluated (Fig. S1). The results showed that all the tested anions had a negligible effect on the fluorescence response of Cr6P1 toward Cr(VI). All the above results demonstrated that Cr6P1 has a high selectivity for Cr(VI) over other competitive metal ions and anions.

Fluorescence spectrometer was used to get the job-plot diagram. The plot of [Cr(VI)]/{[Cr(VI)] + [Cr6P1]} versus the molar fraction of Cr(VI) was provided in Fig. S3, the analysis corresponding to 0.50 the concentration ratio indicates the 1:1 stoichiometric ratio between Cr(VI) and Cr6P1.

Cr(VI) is the highest oxidation state of chromium. Cr(VI) is in the form of dichromate (Cr2O7 2−) in acidic aqueous solutions and chromate (CrO4 2−) in alkaline aqueous solutions. In acidic environments, the oxidation of Cr(VI) is strong, and the oxidation of Cr(VI) is not obvious under neutral or basic conditions [35–37]. In addition, we also investigated the effect of the probe on the recognition of Cr(VI) (0–250 μM) in the reported solution system (CH3CN:HEPES, 30:70, v/v, pH = 7.4, 10 mM) for the recognition of Cu2+ (Fig. S4). The results showed that the probe has no response to Cr(VI) in this solution system, which indicates the acidic system is the key factor for the recognition of Cr(VI).

The most commonly used strong acids are HCl, HNO3 and H2SO4. Concentrated hydrochloric acid can react with Cr2O7 2−, and Cl− may be oxidized to Cl2 and accompanied by the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). If this reaction occurs, the recognition system will lose its oxidizability and cannot prompt the spirocyclic ring opening of the xanthene moiety in the fluorescence probe Cr6P1. Concentrated nitric acid is a strong oxidant and may cause the fluorescence probe Cr6P1 to lose efficacy. Concentrated sulfuric acid also has a strong oxidability, which can also make the fluorescence probe Cr6P1 lose efficacy, and thus, it is not suitable for an identification solution system [38].

The effect of the H2SO4 concentration on the recognition of Cr6P1 toward Cr(VI) was investigated (Fig. S5). There was no fluorescence intensity ratio (F577/F502) when the reaction system lacked H2SO4, which further indicated the important role of the acidic conditions in the oxidation reaction of Cr(VI) toward Cr6P1. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F577/F502) reached its maximum when the concentration of H2SO4 is greater than 10 mM, and at the same time, the probe itself is very stable at the test H2SO4 concentration of 0–90 mM. Therefore, a 10 mM H2SO4 solution was selected as the favorable identification solution system.

A conceivable sensing mechanism was then explored. According to our knowledge, the free probe Cr6P1 contains phenolic oxygen, which is present in equilibrium with its phenol and quinone mesomers in aqueous solution, thus facilitating manipulation of the ESIPT process in the structure of the probe. The oxidation of Cr(VI) under acidic conditions is used to promote the spirocyclic ring opening of the xanthene moiety, leading to the inhibition of the ESIPT process of the fluorescence probe Cr6P1. Herein, a conceivable mechanism of Cr6P1 detection of Cr(VI) was proposed (Scheme 2 ) [23,24,28,39]. To verify the reaction mechanisms, the ESI-HRMS spectra of the final reaction products of Cr6P1 and Cr(VI) were studied. A major ion peak was found at m/z = 521.1526 (Fig. S10), corresponding to the resulting compound 1, which confirmed the proposed mechanism, as shown in Scheme 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of probe Cr6P1 by fluorescent test strip
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the practical application of the probe, we studied the fluorescent test strip of the probe [40–42]. First, the test strip was immersed in the ethanol solution of the probe for 1 min, the strips dried in air and subsequently treated with different concentrations of Cr(VI) (0, 0.1 mM, 1 mM, 10 mM) in 10 mM H2SO4. As shown in the Fig. S11, under the natural light and UV light (365 nm), the colors of the test strip changed significantly with the addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI). The above results showed that the probe can be used in the test strip to detect Cr(VI) with different concentrations quickly and conveniently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: A standard addition experiment was used to investigate the practical application of the probe for the determination of Cr(VI) in drinking water, city tap water and irrigation water [43,44]. As shown in Table 1 , known amounts of Cr(VI) were added to the sample solutions to assess the practicality of the probe Cr6P1. Notably, satisfactory results were observed for the added and detected Cr(VI) contents using the probe Cr6P1. All the recovery values were in the range of 95.8–104%, which demonstrated the applicability of the probe Cr6P1 for the analysis of actual water samples.

In summary, based on strong oxidation of Cr(VI) under acidic conditions, the first ratiometric probe based on small organic fluorescence molecules for the detection of Cr(VI) was developed. The Cr6P1 probe exhibited high selectivity for Cr(VI) over other competitive metal ions (especially Cr(III)) and various coexisting anions in H2SO4 (10 mM), with a low detection limit of 13 nM (6.9 × 10−4 mg/L). In addition, the probe has been successfully applied to monitor Cr(VI) in the analysis of actual water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21907025), Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province of China (192102310140), Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Henan Academy of Agricultural Sciences (2020CX05). We also thank American Journal Experts (www.aje.com) for its linguistic assistance during the preparation of this manuscript (No. T4NR45ZT).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105337.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
